PUBLIC SECTOR PAYMENTS ACT 2025
Signed in Tynwald: 18 February 2025
Received Royal Assent: 18 February 2025
Announced to Tynwald: 18 February 2025
AN ACT to enable payments to holders of certain posts or positions and
members of certain bodies, and for connected purposes.
BE IT ENACTED by the King’s Most Excellent Majesty, by and with the advice and
consent of the Council and Keys in Tynwald assembled, and by the authority of the
same, as follows: —
1 Short title
The short title of this Act is the Public Sector Payments Act 2025.
2 Commencement
(1) This Act commences on the day or days specified in an order made by
Treasury.
(2) An order may specify that this Act commences on different days for
different purposes.
(3) An order may include such consequential, incidental, supplementary,
savings, transitional and transitory provision as the Treasury considers
necessary or expedient.
3 Classes of payee
(1) Prescribed payments may be made to the following classes of person —
(a) members of Tynwald;
(b) members of a Statutory Board;
(c) holders of a prescribed post or position (for example, a non-
executive director or a departmental adviser) of a prescribed
Department: this does not extend to a member of a body referred
to in paragraph (e);
(d) members of a prescribed tribunal;
c AT 1 of 2025 Page 5

Section 4 Public Sector Payments Act 2025
(e) members of a body prescribed as a departmental body;
(f) Electoral Commissioners appointed under section 57 of the
Elections (Keys and Local Authorities) Act 2020.
(2) A prescribed payment may not be paid to the Lord Bishop of the Isle, but
the Treasury may pay the Lord Bishop an amount in lieu of expenses.
4 Prescribed payments
(1) The Treasury may, by regulations, make provision in respect of the
making of prescribed payments to a person to whom a payment may be
made under section 3.
(2) Regulations may make —
(a) the same provision for all persons of the same class referred to in
each of paragraphs (a) to (f) of section 3(1);
(b) different provision for persons in those classes;
(c) no provision for a person in those classes.
(3) Without limiting subsection (1), regulations may make provision with
respect to —
(a) the type and amount of payments;
(b) exemptions and disqualifications from payments;
(c) the period in respect of which payments may be made;
(d) the calculation or determination of payments;
(e) the automatic increase in payments whether by reference to an
index such as the consumer prices index or similar index or
otherwise;
(f) the maximum amount of payments;
(g) the payment of one type of payment in lieu of another;
(h) payments to a person holding more than one post or position;
(i) the commencement of entitlement to a payment;
(j) the cessation of a payment;
(k) the suspension of a payment;
(l) continuation of payment despite loss or cessation of a post or
position;
(m) payments to be made on cessation, expiry or termination of a post
or position;
(n) the functions in respect of which a payment may be made (for
example, attendance at meetings);
(o) circumstances in which, times at which, or the time for which, an
individual is to be treated as, or as not, engaged in carrying out a
function in respect of which a payment may be made;
Page 6 AT 1 of 2025 c

Public Sector Payments Act 2025 Section 5
(p) claims for payment (including way of making a claim, the time for
doing so and the provision of supporting evidence).
(4) A prescribed payment cannot include a payment in respect of travel.
(5) A prescribed payment may be expressed as a monetary amount or by
reference to some other determining factor (for example, pay grades, pay
bands or pay spine points).
(6) A prescribed payment may be expressed as —
(a) an amount between 2 figures (an upper and lower level figure);
(b) an amount expressed by reference to a tier between a lower and
upper level figure.
5 Tax treatment of prescribed payments
A prescribed payment is to be treated as income of the recipient for the purposes
of income tax.
6 Annual report
(1) The Treasury must, in respect of a financial year, prepare an annual report
in respect of payments made under this Act.
(2) Treasury must lay the report before Tynwald as soon as reasonably
practicable after the end of the financial year in question.
(3) The report must, in particular, contain —
(a) a breakdown of the classes of person in respect of which a
prescribed payment has been made;
(b) the total number of persons in each class referred to in
paragraph (a) who have received a prescribed payment (broken
down by class);
(c) the total amount of prescribed payments referred to in
paragraph (b) (broken down by class) together with —
(i) the corresponding amount for the preceding financial year;
(ii) cumulative year on year total;
(d) a list of the types of prescribed payment made under this Act in the
financial year in question;
(e) in the case of prescribed payments which are subject to an
automatic increase or uplift (whether annually or otherwise),
details of —
(i) the amount of the payment prior to its first automatic
increase or uplift in the financial year in question;
(ii) the percentage amount of the increase or uplift; and
(iii) the amount payable as a result of the increase or uplift;
c AT 1 of 2025 Page 7

Section 7 Public Sector Payments Act 2025
(f) any other information the Treasury may prescribe.
(4) A Department, Statutory Board, the General Registry, the Clerk of
Tynwald and an Electoral Commissioner must provide the Treasury with
any information the Treasury considers necessary to enable it to prepare
the report.
(5) “Financial year” means the period beginning on the 1 April and ending on
the following 31 March.
(6) The Treasury may, by regulations, change the dates on which a financial
year begins and ends.
7 Consequential
The Schedule, which contains consequential amendments, has effect.
8 Consultation
For the purpose of making regulations under this Act, the Treasury may —
(a) set up a consultation body;
(b) consult any individual or body (including a committee or sub-
committee or other representative of a body) as it considers
appropriate.
9 Regulations: supplemental
Regulations may make —
(a) supplementary, incidental or consequential provision;
(b) transitory, transitional or saving provision.
10 Tynwald procedure
(1) An order under section 2 is subject to section 34 of the Legislation Act 2015
(laying only).
(2) Regulations under this Act are subject to section 30 of the Legislation
Act 2015 (approval required)
This is subject to subsection (3).
(3) Where the only purpose of regulations is to do one or more of the
following —
(a) record a change of name of person to whom a payment may be
made under this Act;
(b) record a change to the title of the post or position held by a person
to whom a payment may be made under this Act;
(c) remove a person from the list of persons to whom a payment under
this Act may be made;
Page 8 AT 1 of 2025 c

Public Sector Payments Act 2025 Section 11
(d) change the dates on which a financial year begins and ends,
the regulations are subject to section 34 of the Legislation Act 2015 (laying
only).
11 Interpretation
(1) In this Act —
“consumer prices index” means, —
(a) the general index for consumer prices published by Statistics Isle of
Man, Cabinet Office; or
(b) if that index is not published for a relevant month by Statistics Isle
of Man, Cabinet Office —
(i) any substituted index or index figures published by it (or its
successor by whatever name called); or
(ii) the figure for that month published by the Statistics Board
(United Kingdom) for the United Kingdom.
“member of Tynwald” means a member of the Council or the Keys;
“prescribed” (unless otherwise stated) means prescribed in regulations made by
the Treasury: but see subsection (2).
(2) Regulations under section 3(1)(e) prescribing a body as a departmental
body may be made by —
(a) the Treasury;
(b) a Department with the concurrence of the Treasury.
12 Repeal of Payment of Members’ Expenses Act 1989
The Payment of Members’ Expenses Act 1989 is repealed.
This is subject to section 13.
13 Saving and transitional
(1) Where, at the date this Act commences —
(a) a person is entitled to any one or more of the following —
(i) an annual sum under section 1, 2 or 3 of Payment of Members’
Expenses Act 1989;
(ii) an attendance allowance under section 4 of that Act;
(iii) a travelling allowance under section 5 of that Act; and
(b) all or part of such a sum or allowance remains unpaid,
that Act shall continue to apply to that person until the sum or allowance
has been paid in full.
c AT 1 of 2025 Page 9

Section 13 Public Sector Payments Act 2025
(2) Every public document made under the Payment of Members’ Expenses
Act 1989 —
(a) is deemed to have been made under this Act; and
(b) continues in operation despite the repeal of the Payment of Members’
Expenses Act 1989.

TOWN AND COUNTRY PLANNING (AMENDMENT)
ACT 2025
Signed in Tynwald: 17 June 2025
Received Royal Assent: 17 June 2025
Announced to Tynwald: 17 June 2025
AN ACT to amend the Town and Country Planning Act 1999 by expanding the
definition of “development” for the purposes of that Act, to provide for a system
of prior approvals following the grant of planning approval and to provide for
the imposition of fees and charges; and for connected purposes.
BE IT ENACTED by the King’s Most Excellent Majesty, by and with the advice and
consent of the Council and Keys in Tynwald assembled, and by the authority of the
same, as follows: —
PART 1 – INTRODUCTORY
1 Short title
The short title of this Act is the Town and Country Planning (Amendment)
Act 2025.
2 Commencement
(1) This Act (other than section 1 and this section) comes into operation on
such day or days as the Council of Ministers may by order appoint.1
Tynwald procedure — laying only.
(2) An order under subsection (1) may include such consequential, incidental,
supplementary, savings, transitional and transitory provision as the
Council of Ministers consider necessary or expedient.
c AT 3 of 2025 Page 5

Section 3 Town and Country Planning (Amendment) Act 2025
PART 2 – TOWN AND COUNTRY PLANNING ACT 1999
AMENDED
3 Town and Country Planning Act 1999 amended
The Town and Country Planning Act 1999 is amended as follows.
4 Amendment of section 2

(2) [Amended subsection (7)]
(3) [Inserted subsections (8) to (12)]
5 New section 2ZA
After section 2 (development plan), insert —
«2ZA Mistakes in maps
(1) The Cabinet Office must, by regulations, make provision in respect
of —
(a) the correction of a mistake in a map referred to in section 2;
and
(b) the payment by a prescribed person or body of
compensation to, or in respect, of a prescribed person.
The matters in paragraphs (a) and (b) may be the subject of separate
instruments.
(2) For the purposes of this section, a “mistake” does not include a
failure to indicate land which has been allocated for development
on a map.
(3) Without limiting subsection (1), regulations under this section
must make provision about, —
(a) the period during which a mistake may be corrected;
(b) what constitutes a mistake;
(c) the procedure to be followed in correcting or otherwise
dealing with a mistake;
(d) the publication of corrections and corrected maps and
associated documents.
(4) Without limiting subsection (1), regulations under this section may
make provision about, —
(a) the effect of making a correction;
Page 6 AT 3 of 2025 c

Town and Country Planning (Amendment) Act 2025 Section 6
(b) the discharge of prescribed functions under the regulations
by a prescribed person;
(c) the manner in which any claim for compensation is to be
made;
(d) the purpose, and calculation, of compensation;
(e) reviews in respect of decisions on a claim for compensation
including who is to exercise functions in relation to a review
and their powers on a review;
(f) time limits for making a claim for compensation.
(5) The period referred to in subsection (3)(a) —
(a) commences with the Cabinet Office’s publication of the
uncorrected plan in accordance with paragraph 7 of
Schedule 1; and
(b) cannot be longer than 3 years.
(6) Publication referred to in subsection (3)(d) must be effective to
bring the correction to the attention of those the Cabinet Office
considers most likely to be affected by it (including those who may
not be the owners of the land in respect of which a correction is
made).
(7) Before making regulations under this section, the Cabinet Office
must consult the persons likely to be affected by them as it
considers appropriate.
This does not affect the Cabinet Office’s duty under section 43A.
2ZB Mistakes in maps: supplementary
Where a correction to a map is made under section 2ZA, the corrected map
shall not have effect as part of the relevant strategic plan or area plan
unless —
(a) the corrected map is adopted by the Cabinet Office by order
which must be laid before Tynwald; and
(b) Tynwald, at the sitting before which it is laid or the next
following sitting, does not resolve that the order should be
annulled.».

«42A Restrictions on Department dealing with applications
(1) This subsection applies to an application —
(a) for planning approval or registered building consent;
(b) for prior approval;
(c) to vary or discharge the conditions attached to a planning
approval or registered building consent;
(d) for the approval of matters reserved in a planning
application for subsequent approval;

(f) for minor changes to a planning approval;
(g) for the display of advertisements or any related approval or
consent;

(which deals with the issue of certificates relating to the
lawfulness of use or development).
(2) An application referred to in subsection (1) may not be dealt with
by any officer of the Department who is wholly or partly
responsible for the management of any land or buildings to which
the application relates (but may be dealt with by any other officer
of the Department).
(3) An application referred to in subsection (1) may not be dealt with
by the Minister for the Department or any person appointed a
member of the Department pursuant to section 2 of the Government
Departments Act 1987.
(4) Nothing in this section affects section 39C.».

(1) In this section “the 1999 Act” means the Town and Country Planning
Act 1999.
(2) Subsection (3) applies to an application, made before this Act comes into
operation, for which approval under Part 2 of the 1999 Act and consent
under section 19 of that Act were both required.
(3) If in respect of an application referred to in subsection (2) —
(a) the Department has given consent under section 19 of the 1999 Act,
that consent shall remain valid;
(b) that application has not been disposed of before section 16 of this
Act comes into operation, section 19 of 1999 Act as it applied
immediately before the coming into operation of section 16 of this
Act continues to apply to such an application; and
(c) that application has not been disposed of under Part 2 of the 1999
Act before this Act comes into operation —
Page 12 AT 3 of 2025 c

Town and Country Planning (Amendment) Act 2025 Section 24
(i) Part 2 of the 1999 Act continues to apply to such an
application as it applied immediately before the coming into
operation of this Act; and
(ii) section 8(2)(c) of this Act does not affect such an application.
(4) Subsection (5) applies to an application made before this Act comes into
operation for which approval under Part 2 of the 1999 Act was not
required but consent under section 19 of that Act was.
(5) If in respect of an application referred to in subsection (4) —
(a) the Department has given consent under section 19 of the 1999 Act
prior to section 16 of this Act coming into operation, that consent
shall remain valid;
(b) the application has not been disposed of before section 16 of this
Act comes into operation, section 19 of 1999 Act as it applied
immediately before the coming into operation of section 16 of this
Act continues to apply to such an application; and
(c) sections 8(2)(c) and 16 of this Act shall not otherwise affect such an
application.
(6) Subsection (7) applies where an application for planning approval —
(a) was made before section 8(2)(c) of this Act comes into operation;
(b) was made in respect of an area which is not a conservation area;
and
(c) involves the demolition in that area of all of a building which is not
attached to any other building.
(7) Where this subsection applies —
(a) section 8(2)(c) of this Act does not affect that application; and
(b) section 6(2)(e) of the 1999 Act as it applied immediately before the
coming into operation of section 8(2)(c) of this Act continues to
apply to the approval of that application.
(8) Where an application under paragraph 2(2) of Schedule 2 to the 1999 Act
has been made, but not disposed of, before section 23(3) of this Act comes
into operation —
(a) that application shall be dealt with under that paragraph as it was
in operation immediately before section 23(3) of this Act comes into

(b) section 23(3) of this Act does not affect that application.
(9) It is irrelevant for the purposes of this section whether or not an

REPRODUCTIVE RIGHTS ACT 2025
Signed in Tynwald: 21 October 2025
Received Royal Assent: 21 October 2025
Announced to Tynwald: 21 October 2025
AN ACT to make certain new provision in relation to fertilisation, embryology
and surrogacy; to make new provision about the persons who in certain
circumstances are to be treated as the parents of a child; to amend the Civil
Registration Act 1984 consequentially in relation to registering births; and for
connected purposes.
BE IT ENACTED by the King’s Most Excellent Majesty, by and with the advice and
consent of the Council and Keys in Tynwald assembled, and by the authority of the
same, as follows: —
PART 1 – INTRODUCTORY
1 Short title
The short title of this Act is the Reproductive Rights Act 2025.
2 Commencement
(1) This Act, apart from section 1, this section and the definition of
“Department” in section 4(1), comes into operation on such day or days as
the Department may by order appoint.
Tynwald procedure – laying only.
(2) An order under subsection (1) may include such supplemental, incidental,
consequential and transitional provisions as appear to the Department to
be necessary or expedient.
3 Meaning of “embryo”, “gamete” and associated expressions
P1990/37/1 and drafting
(1) In this Act (except in section 12 or in the term “human admixed
embryo”) —
c AT 6 of 2025 Page 7

Section 4 Reproductive Rights Act 2025
(a) embryo means a live human embryo and does not include a human
admixed embryo (as defined by section 12(6)); and
(b) references to an embryo include an egg that is in the process of
fertilisation or is undergoing any other process capable of resulting
in an embryo.
(2) This Act, so far as it governs bringing about the creation of an embryo,
applies only to bringing about the creation of an embryo outside the
human body; and in this Act —
(a) references to embryos the creation of which was brought about in
vitro (in their application to those where fertilisation or any other
process by which an embryo is created is complete) are to those
where fertilisation or any other process by which the embryo was
created began outside the human body; and
(b) references to embryos taken from a woman do not include embryos
whose creation was brought about in vitro.
(3) This Act, so far as it governs the keeping or use of an embryo, applies only
to keeping or using an embryo outside the human body.
(4) In this Act (except in section 12) —
(a) references to eggs are to live human eggs which could be fertilised,
including cells of the female germ line at any stage of maturity, but
(except in subsection (1)(b)) not including eggs that are in the
process of fertilisation or are undergoing any other process capable
of resulting in an embryo;
(b) references to sperm are to human sperm which could fertilise,
including cells of the male germ line at any stage of maturity; and
(c) references to gametes are to be read accordingly.
(5) For the purposes of this Act, sperm is to be treated as partner-donated
sperm if the donor of the sperm and the recipient of the sperm declare that
they have an intimate physical relationship.
(6) If it appears to the Department necessary or desirable to do so in the light
of developments in science or medicine, the Department may by
regulations provide that in this Act (except in section 12) “embryo”,
“eggs”, “sperm” or “gametes” includes things specified in the regulations
which would not otherwise fall within the definition.
Tynwald procedure ‒approval required.
(7) Regulations made by virtue of subsection (6) may not provide for
anything containing any nuclear or mitochondrial DNA that is not human
to be treated as an embryo or as eggs, sperm or gametes.
4 Interpretation: other terms
(1) In this Act —
Page 8 AT 6 of 2025 c

Reproductive Rights Act 2025 Section 4
“conception services” means medical, surgical or obstetric services provided for
the purpose of assisting women to conceive;
“Department” means the Department of Health and Social Care;
“embryo” has the meaning given by section 3;
“female germ cells” means cells of the female germ line and includes such cells
at any stage of maturity and accordingly includes eggs;
“human admixed embryo” has the meaning given by section 12(6);
“human application” means use in a human recipient;
“licence” means a licence issued pursuant to an order under section 14;
“non-medical conception services” means any services that are provided, in the
course of a business, for the purpose of assisting women to conceive, but
are not medical, surgical or obstetric services;
“payment” means payment in money or money’s worth;
“person responsible” —
(a) in relation to a licence, is to be construed in accordance with an
order under section 14; and
(b) in relation to a UK licence, is to be construed in accordance with
section 17 of the Human Fertilisation and Embryology Act 1990 (of
Parliament);
“processing”, in relation to gametes or embryos intended for human application,
means any operation involved in their preparation, manipulation or
packaging, and related terms are to be interpreted accordingly;
“prohibited degrees of relationship” has the meaning given by section 17;
“store”, in relation to gametes, embryos or human admixed embryos, means
preserve, whether by cryopreservation or in any other way, and “storage”
and “stored” are to be interpreted accordingly;
“UK licence” means a licence issued pursuant to Schedule 2 to the Human
Fertilisation and Embryology Act 1990 (of Parliament) or pursuant to any
provision of UK legislation amending, varying or modifying that Schedule
directly or indirectly.
(2) References in Part 2 to keeping, in relation to embryos, gametes or human
admixed embryos, include keeping while preserved in storage.
(3) For the purposes of this Act, a woman is not to be treated as carrying a
child until the embryo has become implanted.
c AT 6 of 2025 Page 9

Section 5 Reproductive Rights Act 2025
5 Conscientious objection
P1990/37/38
(1) No person who has a conscientious objection to participating in any
activity governed by Part 2 shall be under any duty, however arising, to
do so.
(2) In any legal proceedings, a statement in writing and on oath or affirmation
by a person that the person has a conscientious objection to participating
in any activity governed by Part 2 is conclusive evidence of that objection.
(3) A person referred to in subsection (1) must not —
(a) have the person’s employment terminated; or
(b) be treated less favourably in the course of that employment,
as a consequence of having a conscientious objection or otherwise not
participating in activity governed by Part 2.
PART 2 – HUMAN FERTILISATION AND EMBRYOLOGY
Departmental functions and procedure
6 General functions of the Department
P1990/37/8 and drafting
(1) In this Part, activities regulated by sections 8 to 12 are referred to as
“activities governed by this Part”.
(2) The Department shall —
(a) keep under review information about embryos and any subsequent
development of embryos and about the provision of conception
services and activities governed by this Part;
(b) publicise the services provided to the public by the Department or
provided in pursuance of licences;
(c) provide, to such extent as it considers appropriate, advice and
information for persons to whom licences apply or who are
receiving conception services or providing gametes or embryos for
use for the purposes of activities governed by this Part, or may wish
to do so;
(d) maintain a statement of the general principles which it considers
should be followed —
(i) in the carrying-on of activities governed by this Part; and
(ii) in the carrying-out of its functions in relation to such
activities;
(e) promote, in relation to activities governed by this Part, compliance
with —
Page 10 AT 6 of 2025 c

Reproductive Rights Act 2025 Section 7
(i) requirements imposed by or under this Act; and
(ii) the code of practice under section 7; and
(f) perform such other functions as may be specified in regulations
made by the Department.
Tynwald procedure – approval required.
(3) The Department may by regulations prescribe fees which may be levied
by the Department or by licensees in relation to any of the activities
governed by this Part or in relation to the issuing of licences under this
Part.
Tynwald procedure – approval required.
(4) The Department shall carry out its functions effectively, efficiently and
economically.
(5) In carrying out its functions, the Department shall, so far as relevant, have
regard to the principles of best regulatory practice (including the
principles under which regulatory activities should be transparent,
accountable, proportionate, consistent and targeted only at cases in which
action is needed).
7 Code of practice
P1990/37/25 and drafting
(1) The Department shall maintain a code of practice giving guidance about
the proper conduct of activities carried on in pursuance of a licence under
this Part and the proper discharge of the functions of the person
responsible and other persons to whom the licence applies.
Tynwald procedure ‒ laying only.
(2) The guidance given by the code shall include guidance for those providing
conception services about the account to be taken of the welfare of children
who may be born as a result of conception services (including a child’s
need for supportive parenting), and of other children who may be affected
by such births.
(3) The code shall also give guidance about —
(a) the giving of a suitable opportunity to receive proper counselling;
and
(b) the provision of such relevant information as is proper,
in accordance with a licence granted in accordance with section 14(1).
(4) The code may also give guidance about the use of any technique involving
the placing of sperm and eggs in a woman.
(5) The Department may from time to time revise the whole or any part of the
code.
Tynwald procedure ‒ laying only.
c AT 6 of 2025 Page 11

Section 8 Reproductive Rights Act 2025
(6) The Department shall publish the code as for the time being in operation.
(7) A failure on the part of any person to observe any provision of the code
shall not of itself render the person liable to any proceedings, but —
(a) the Department shall, in considering whether there has been any
failure to comply with any conditions of a licence and, in particular,
conditions requiring anything to be “proper” or “suitable”, take
account of any relevant provision of the code; and
(b) the Department may, in considering, where it has power to do so,
whether or not to vary or revoke a licence, take into account any
observance of or failure to observe the provisions of the code.
Activities governed by this Part
8 Prohibitions in connection with embryos
P1990/37/3 and drafting
(1) No person shall bring about the creation of an embryo except in pursuance
of a licence.
(2) No person shall keep or use an embryo except in pursuance of a licence.
(3) No person shall procure or distribute an embryo intended for human
application except in pursuance of a licence.
(4) No person shall place in a woman —
(a) an embryo other than a permitted embryo (as defined by section 9);
or
(b) any gametes other than permitted eggs or permitted sperm (as
defined in section 9).
(5) A licence cannot authorise —
(a) keeping or using an embryo after the appearance of the primitive
streak;
(b) placing an embryo in any animal; or
(c) keeping or using an embryo in any circumstances in which
regulations made by the Department prohibit its keeping or use.
Tynwald procedure – approval required.
(6) For the purposes of subsection (5)(a), the primitive streak is to be taken to
have appeared in an embryo not later than the end of the period of 14 days
beginning with the day on which the process of creating the embryo
began, not counting any time during which the embryo is stored.
(7) If it appears to the Department necessary or desirable to do so in the light
of developments in science or medicine, the Department may by
regulations vary the period of days specified in subsection (6).
Tynwald procedure – approval required.
Page 12 AT 6 of 2025 c

Reproductive Rights Act 2025 Section 9
9 Permitted eggs, permitted sperm and permitted embryos
P1990/37/3ZA and drafting
(1) This section has effect for the interpretation of section 8(4).
(2) A permitted egg is one —
(a) which has been produced by or extracted from the ovaries of a
woman; and
(b) whose nuclear or mitochondrial DNA has not been altered.
(3) Permitted sperm are sperm —
(a) which have been produced by or extracted from the testes of a man;
and
(b) whose nuclear or mitochondrial DNA has not been altered.
(4) An embryo is a permitted embryo if —
(a) it has been created by the fertilisation of a permitted egg by
permitted sperm;
(b) no nuclear or mitochondrial DNA of any cell of the embryo has
been altered; and
(c) no cell has been added to it other than by division of the embryo’s
own cells.
(5) The Department may by regulations provide that —
(a) an egg can be a permitted egg; or
(b) an embryo can be a permitted embryo,
even though the egg or embryo has had applied to it in prescribed
circumstances a prescribed process designed to prevent the transmission
of serious mitochondrial disease.
Tynwald procedure – approval required.
(6) In this section —
(a) “woman” and “man” include respectively a girl and a boy (from
birth); and
(b) “prescribed” means prescribed by regulations made under
subsection (5).
10 Prohibition in connection with germ cells
P1990/37/3A and drafting
No person shall, for the purpose of providing conception services for any woman,
use female germ cells taken or derived from an embryo or a foetus or use embryos
created by using such cells.
c AT 6 of 2025 Page 13

Section 11 Reproductive Rights Act 2025
11 Prohibitions in connection with gametes
P1990/37/4 and drafting
(1) No person shall —
(a) store any gametes; or
(b) in the course of providing conception services for any woman,
use —
(i) any sperm, other than partner-donated sperm which has
been neither processed nor stored;
(ii) the woman’s eggs after processing or storage; or
(iii) the eggs of any other woman,
except in pursuance of a licence.
(2) No person shall procure, test, process or distribute any gametes intended
for human application except in pursuance of a licence.
(3) A licence cannot authorise storing or using gametes in any circumstances
in which regulations made by the Department prohibit their storage or
use.
Tynwald procedure – approval required.
(4) No person shall place sperm and eggs in a woman in any circumstances
specified in regulations made by the Department except in pursuance of a
licence.
Tynwald procedure – approval required.
12 Prohibitions in connection with genetic material not of human origin
P1990/37/4A and drafting
(1) No person shall place in a woman —
(a) a human admixed embryo;
(b) any other embryo that is not a human embryo; or
(c) any gametes other than human gametes.
(2) No person shall —
(a) mix human gametes with animal gametes;
(b) bring about the creation of a human admixed embryo; or
(c) keep or use a human admixed embryo,
except in pursuance of a licence.
(3) A licence cannot authorise keeping or using a human admixed embryo
after the earliest of the following —
(a) the appearance of the primitive streak; or
(b) the end of the period of 14 days beginning with the day on which
the process of creating the human admixed embryo began, but not

counting any time during which the human admixed embryo is
stored.
(4) A licence cannot authorise placing a human admixed embryo in an animal.
(5) A licence cannot authorise keeping or using a human admixed embryo in
any circumstances in which regulations made by the Department prohibit
its keeping or use.
Tynwald procedure – approval required.
(6) For the purposes of this Act a human admixed embryo is —
(a) an embryo created by replacing the nucleus of an animal egg or of
an animal cell, or two animal pronuclei, with —
(i) two human pronuclei;
(ii) one nucleus of a human gamete or of any other human cell;
or
(iii) one human gamete or other human cell;
(b) any other embryo created by using —
(i) human gametes and animal gametes; or
(ii) one human pronucleus and one animal pronucleus;
(c) a human embryo that has been altered by the introduction of any
sequence of nuclear or mitochondrial DNA of an animal into one
or more cells of the embryo;
(d) a human embryo that has been altered by the introduction of one
or more animal cells; or
(e) any embryo not falling within paragraphs (a) to (d) which contains
both nuclear or mitochondrial DNA of a human and nuclear or
mitochondrial DNA of an animal (“animal DNA”) but in which the
animal DNA is not predominant.
(7) In subsection (6) —
(a) references to animal cells are to cells of an animal or of an animal
embryo; and
(b) references to human cells are to cells of a human or of a human
embryo.
(8) For the purposes of this section an “animal” is an animal other than a
human.
(9) In this section “embryo” means a live embryo, including an egg that is in
the process of fertilisation or is undergoing any other process capable of
resulting in an embryo.
(10) In this section —
(a) references to eggs are to live eggs, including cells of the female
germ line at any stage of maturity, but (except in subsection (9)) not
including eggs that are in the process of fertilisation or are

PART 4 – SURROGACY ARRANGEMENTS
43 Part 4 interpretation
(1) In this Part —
“surrogacy arrangement” shall be construed in accordance with subsection (2);
“surrogate mother” means a woman who carries a child in pursuance of an
arrangement —
(a) made before she began to carry the child; and
(b) made with a view to any child carried in pursuance of it being
handed over to, and parental responsibility being met (so far as
practicable) by, another person or other persons.
(2) For the purposes of this Part —
(a) if a woman were to carry a child in pursuance of an arrangement
and thereby became a surrogate mother, the arrangement is a
surrogacy arrangement;
(b) in determining whether an arrangement is made with such a view
as is mentioned in paragraph (b) of the definition of “surrogate
mother”, regard may be had to the circumstances as a whole (and,
in particular, where there is a promise or understanding that any
payment will or may be made to the woman or for her benefit in
respect of the carrying of the child in pursuance of the arrangement,
to that promise or understanding);
(c) an arrangement may be regarded as made with such a view
although it is subject to conditions regarding the handing over of
the child;
(d) a woman who carries a child is to be treated for the purposes of the
definition of “surrogate mother” as beginning to carry it at the time
of the insemination or of the placing in her of an embryo, of an egg
in the process of fertilisation or of sperm and eggs, as the case may
be, that results in her carrying the child.
(3) Subject to section 45(8), for the purposes of this Part a person does an act
on a commercial basis if —
(a) any payment is at any time received by the person or another in
respect of it; or

(4) Sections 45 to 47 apply to arrangements whether or not they are lawful.

(a) for a woman, with a view to becoming a surrogate mother herself,

(b) for any person, with a view to a surrogate mother carrying a child
for the person, to do such an act or to cause such an act to be done.

(a) the body does an act falling within subsection (1)(a) or (c) in respect

(b) it does an act falling within subsection (1)(a) or (c) with a view to

of facilitating the making of any surrogacy arrangement.

ACT 2001 (AS SAVED)
[Section 50(4)]
86 Meaning of “mother”
[P1990/37/27]
(1) The woman who is carrying or has carried a child as a result of the placing
in her of an embryo or of sperm and eggs, and no other woman, is to be
treated as the mother of the child.
(2) Subsection (1) does not apply to any child to the extent that the child is
treated by virtue of adoption as not being the child of any person other
than the adopter or adopters.
(3) For the purposes of this section it is immaterial where the woman was at
the time of the placing in her of the embryo or the sperm and eggs.
87 Meaning of “father”
[P1990/37/28]
(1) This section applies in the case of a child who is being or has been carried
by a woman as the result of the placing in her of an embryo or of sperm
and eggs or her artificial insemination.
(2) If —
(a) at the time of the placing in her of the embryo or the sperm and
eggs or of her insemination, the woman was a party to a marriage
or in a civil partnership with a man, and
(b) the creation of the embryo carried by her was not brought about
with the sperm of her husband or her civil partner,
then, subject to subsection (5), her husband or her civil partner shall be
treated as the father of the child unless it is shown that he did not consent
to the placing in her of the embryo or the sperm and eggs or to her
insemination (as the case may be).
(3) If no man is treated, by virtue of subsection (2), as the father of the child
but —
(a) the embryo or the sperm and eggs were placed in the woman, or
she was artificially inseminated, in the course of fertility services
provided for her and a man together, and
(b) the creation of the embryo carried by her was not brought about
with the sperm of that man,
then, subject to subsection (5), that man shall be treated as the father of the
child.
Page 54 AT 6 of 2025 c

Reproductive Rights Act 2025 SCHEDULE 2
(4) Where a person is treated as the father of the child by virtue of
subsection (2) or (3), no other person is to be treated as the father of the
child.
(5) Subsections (2) and (3) do not apply —
(a) to any child who, by virtue of the rules of common law, is treated
as the legitimate child of the parties to a marriage, or
(b) to any child to the extent that the child is treated by virtue of
adoption as not being the child of any person other than the
adopter or adopters.
(6) Where the sperm of a man, or any embryo the creation of which was
brought about with his sperm, was used after his death, he is not to be
treated as the father of the child.
(7) The reference in subsection (2) to the parties to a marriage at the time there
referred to —
(a) is to the parties to a marriage subsisting at that time, unless a
judicial separation was then in force, but
(b) includes the parties to a void marriage if either or both of them
reasonably believed at the time that the marriage was valid;
and for the purposes of this subsection it shall be presumed, unless the
contrary is shown, that one of them reasonably believed at the time that
the marriage was valid.
(7A) The reference in subsection (2) to the woman being in a civil partnership
with a man at the time there referred to —
(a) is to the man and the woman being in a civil partnership subsisting
at that time, unless a judicial separation was then in force, but
(b) includes the parties to a void civil partnership between the man
and the woman if either or both of them reasonably believed at the
time that the civil partnership was valid,
and for the purposes of this subsection it shall be presumed, unless the
contrary is shown, that one of them reasonably believed at the time that
the civil partnership was valid.
(8) For the purposes of this section it is immaterial where the woman was at
the time of the placing in her of the embryo or the sperm and eggs or her
artificial insemination.
(9) In subsection (7)(a) “judicial separation” includes a legal separation
obtained in a country outside the British Islands and recognised in the
Island.

SCHEDULE 2 Reproductive Rights Act 2025
88 Effect of sections 86 and 87
[P1990/37/29]
(1) Where by virtue of section 86 or 87 a person is to be treated as the mother
or father of a child, that person is to be treated in law as the mother or, as
the case may be, the father of the child for all purposes.
(2) Where by virtue of section 86 or 87 a person is not to be treated as the
mother or father of a child, that person is to be treated in law as not being
the mother or, as the case may be, the father of the child for any purpose.
(3) Where subsection (1) or (2) has effect, references to any relationship
between 2 people in any statutory provision, deed or other instrument or
document (whenever made) are to be read accordingly.
(4) Nothing in section 86(1) or 87(2) to (4), read with this section, affects —
(a) the succession to any dignity or title of honour or renders any
person capable of succeeding to or transmitting a right to succeed
to any such dignity or title, or
(b) the devolution of any property limited (expressly or not) to devolve
(as nearly as the law permits) along with any dignity or title of
honour.
(5) Sections 86 and 87 and this section have effect only in relation to children
carried by women as a result of the placing in them of embryos or of sperm
or eggs, or of their artificial insemination (as the case may be), after the
commencement of this section.

CATHEDRAL MEASURE (ISLE OF MAN) 2025
Approved by Sodor and Man Diocesan Synod: 28 November 2023
Approved by Tynwald: 22 January 2025
Received Royal Assent: 8 July 2025
Announced to Tynwald: 14 July 2025
A MEASURE enacted pursuant to the Church Legislation Procedure Act 1993 to
make provision about the governance, management, property and financial
affairs of the Cathedral Church of Saint German; and for connected purposes.
Introductory
1 Short title
The short title of this Measure is the Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025.
2 Commencement
(1) This Measure comes into operation on such day or days as the Bishop may
by order appoint.1
(2) An order under subsection (1) may include such transitional provisions as
appear to the Bishop to be necessary or expedient for the purposes of the
order.
3 Interpretation
In this Measure —
“the appointed day”, in relation to any provision of this Measure, means the day
on which that provision comes into operation;
“barred list” has the same meaning as in the Safeguarding Vulnerable Groups
Act 2006 (an Act of Parliament);
“the Board” means the Sodor and Man Diocesan Board of Finance;
“canon” (without more) means a canon appointed by the Bishop under the
constitution;
c SM 1 of 2025 Page 5

Section 4 Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025
“the Cathedral” means the Cathedral Church of Saint German at Peel;
“the Cathedral benefice” means the benefice (formerly named the benefice of the
West Coast) created by the Western Pastoral Scheme 2012;
“the Cathedral community” means, —
(a) persons over the age of 16 who worship regularly in the Cathedral
or are engaged in work or service connected with the Cathedral in
a regular capacity, and
(b) such other persons as the statutes may specify as members of the
Cathedral community;
“the Cathedral parish” means the parish so named and created by the Cathedral
Pastoral Scheme 2023;
“the Chapter” means the Chapter of the Cathedral constituted under Schedule 1;
“the clergy of the Cathedral” means each clerk in Holy Orders who carries out
duties (whether in the Cathedral or the diocese) which should, in the
opinion of the Chapter after consultation with the Bishop, be performed
in or from the Cathedral;
“chief officer” means an officer appointed under paragraph 6 of Schedule 2;
“child” means a person aged under 18;
“the community roll” means the roll maintained under paragraph 10 of
Schedule 1;
“consent” means written consent;
“the constitution” means the constitution of the Chapter for the time being in
force under Schedule 1;
“the diocese” means the diocese of Sodor and Man;
“elected member”, in relation to the Chapter, means a member elected under
paragraph 5 of Schedule 1;
“property” includes a thing in action and any interest in real or personal
property;
“the statutes” means the statutes for the time being in force under Schedule 2;
“vulnerable adult” has the meaning given by subsection (2) of section 6 of the
Safeguarding and Clergy Discipline Measure 2016 (subject to
subsections (3) to (6) of that section).
Status and purpose
4 Status and purpose of Cathedral
(1) The Cathedral continues to be —
(a) the cathedral church for the diocese, and
Page 6 SM 1 of 2025 c

Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025 Section 5
(b) the parish church of the Cathedral parish.
(2) A person on whom a function is conferred by or under this Measure must,
in exercising that function, have regard to, —
(a) the fact that the Cathedral is the seat of the Bishop and a centre of
worship and mission, and
(b) the importance of the Cathedral’s role in providing a focus for the
life and work of the Church of England in the diocese.
The Chapter
5 Constitution of the Chapter
(1) The Chapter of the Cathedral established by section 4 of the Church
Act 1895 is to continue to exist.
(2) The Chapter continues to be a body corporate with perpetual succession
and a common seal.
(3) Schedule 1 has effect with respect to the constitution of the Chapter.
6 Statutes
Schedule 2 has effect with respect to the statutes of the Cathedral.
Roles and responsibilities
7 The Bishop
(1) The Bishop has the principal seat and dignity in the Cathedral.
(2) The Bishop may, after consultation with the Chapter and subject to
provision in the statutes, officiate in the Cathedral and use it in the
Bishop’s work of teaching and mission, for ordinations and synods and
for other diocesan occasions and purposes.
(3) The Bishop must attend the special meeting of the Chapter held under
section 9(3).
(4) The Bishop may seek the advice of the Chapter on any matter.
(5) The Bishop may commission a review of such aspects of the Cathedral’s
financial affairs, governance, management, operations or mission as the
Bishop considers necessary or appropriate.
(6) (6)Where a review under subsection (5) is being carried out, the Chapter
must co-operate with the persons carrying it out.
(7) The Chapter must, in exercising its functions, have due regard to the
conclusions reached on a review under subsection (5) and any
recommendations made in the review.
c SM 1 of 2025 Page 7

Section 8 Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025
8 The Visitor
(1) The constitution must provide for the Bishop to be the Visitor of the
Cathedral.
(2) It is for the Bishop as Visitor to hear and determine any question as to the
construction of the constitution or statutes.
(3) The Bishop may hold a visitation of the Cathedral, —
(a) when the Bishop considers it necessary or desirable to do so, or
(b) when requested to do so by the Chapter.
(4) In the course of a visitation, the Bishop may give such directions to the
Chapter, the holder of an office in the Cathedral or a person employed by
the Chapter or engaged to provide services in connection with the
Cathedral as will, in the opinion of the Bishop, better serve the due
observance of the constitution and statutes.
(5) A person on whom a function is conferred by or under this Measure must,
in exercising that function, act in accordance with a determination made
under subsection (2) or a direction given under subsection (4).
(6) This section does not affect the powers that the Bishop has generally as
Visitor.
9 The Chapter
(1) The Chapter must direct and oversee the administration of the affairs of
the Cathedral.
(2) In performing that duty the Chapter must in particular, —
(a) order the worship of the Cathedral and promote its mission;
(b) formulate, after consultation with the Bishop, proposals relating to
the general direction and mission of the Cathedral;
(c) prepare an annual budget for the Chapter;
(d) prepare an annual report for the Chapter;
(e) keep under review the constitution and statutes;
(f) manage the property vested in the Chapter and the income
accruing from it; and
(g) ensure that necessary repairs and maintenance in respect of the
Cathedral and its contents and other buildings and monuments are
carried out.
(3) The Chapter must each year hold a special meeting at which, —
(a) the Chapter must consult the Bishop on the general direction and
mission of the Cathedral, and
(b) the Bishop may seek the advice of the Chapter on any particular
matter.
Page 8 SM 1 of 2025 c

Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025 Section 10
(4) Before deciding whom to invite to a special meeting under subsection (3)
(apart from the members of the Chapter and the Bishop), the Chapter must
consult the Bishop.
10 The Dean
(1) The Dean is the principal dignitary of the Cathedral, next after the Bishop.
(2) The Dean, in that capacity, must govern and direct on behalf of the
Chapter the life and work of the Cathedral.
(3) In performing that duty the Dean must in particular, —
(a) ensure that Divine Service is duly performed in the Cathedral;
(b) ensure that the constitution and statutes are faithfully observed;
(c) oversee the work undertaken by the clergy and staff of the
Cathedral in relation to the Cathedral;
(d) maintain good order and proper reverence in the Cathedral;
(e) secure the pastoral care of all members of the Cathedral
community;
(f) provide leadership on matters relating to the safeguarding of any
children or vulnerable adults who work, volunteer or worship in
the Cathedral or who visit the Cathedral;
(g) take all decisions necessary to deal with an emergency affecting the
Cathedral, pending consideration of the matter by the Chapter.
(4) The Dean is, in respect of his or her functions relating to the Cathedral,
accountable to the Chapter in accordance with arrangements made by the
Chapter.
(5) The clergy of the Cathedral are, in respect of their functions relating to the
Cathedral, accountable to the Chapter through the Dean in accordance
with arrangements made by the Chapter.
Finance and property
11 Accounts of the Chapter
(1) The Chapter must cause —
(a) its accounts, including the accounts of all trusts administered by it,
for each year ending on 31 December, and
(b) a statement of the funds and property, if any, remaining in the
hands of the Chapter at that date,
to be made up and examined in accordance with subsection (2).
(2) The accounts and statement referred to in subsection (1) must be examined
by an independent person who, —
c SM 1 of 2025 Page 9

Section 12 Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025
(a) is an accountant;
(b) holds a qualification prescribed by regulations under section 46 of
the Charities Registration and Regulation Act 2019; or
(c) is for the time being approved by the Bishop for the purpose of this
subsection.
(3) The Chapter must —
(a) present to the annual parochial church meeting of the Cathedral
parish —
(i) its accounts, including the accounts of all trusts
administered by it, for the year ending on the 31 December
immediately preceding the meeting, and
(ii) a statement of the funds and property, if any, remaining in
the hands of the Chapter at that date;
(b) not later than 7 days before the annual parochial church meeting
cause a copy of the examined accounts and statement to be
published and affixed for a period of at least 14 days on or near the
principal door of the Cathedral, and
(c) not later than 14 days after the annual parochial church meeting
send a copy of the examined accounts and statement to the Board.
(4) No value is to be given for the Cathedral in any statement of the funds and
property of the Chapter.
12 Acquisition and disposal of land
(1) The Chapter may not acquire land without the consent of the Board.
(2) Consent under subsection (1) is not required for the acquisition of land by
gift or will.
(3) The Chapter may disclaim land which it would otherwise acquire by gift
or will, but only with the consent of the Board.
(4) Schedule 2 to the Church Act 1992 (disposal of Church land) applies to the
Chapter as it applies to the incumbent of a benefice.
13 Transfer of Cathedral church etc.
On the appointed day the Cathedral and the land and premises occupied with it
(including the Corrin Hall), comprised in —
(a) two conveyances by Elizabeth Jane Graves dated 15 August 1879
and 31 January 1889. and
(b) a conveyance made 25 May 1921 by the Council of Ediucation,
are transferred to the Chapter.
Page 10 SM 1 of 2025 c

Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025 Section 14
14 Transfer of functions etc. of PCC
(1) On the appointed day the functions of the parochial church council of the
Cathedral parish (“the council”) are transferred to the Chapter, and the
council is dissolved.
(2) On the appointed day —
(a) all property which immediately before the appointed day was
vested in, held by, or held on trust for the council is transferred to
the Chapter;
(b) all rights, obligations and liabilities which immediately before the
appointed day were enforceable by or against the council are on
and after the transfer to be enforceable by or against the Chapter;
(c) subject to Schedules 3 and 4, all references to the council or its
secretary in any statutory provision, agreement, deed, instrument,
licence, consent, application, notice or other document whatsoever
are to be construed as references to the Chapter or its clerk;
(d) all persons who immediately before the appointed day were
employed by or in the service of the council are on and after the
appointed day to be deemed to be employed by or in the service of
the Chapter without further appointment on and subject to the like
terms and conditions as those on and subject to which they were
employed immediately before the appointed day;
(e) the transfer does not affect the validity of anything done by or in
relation to the council before the appointed day; and anything
which on the appointed day is in the process of being done by or in
relation to the council may be continued by or in relation to the
Chapter;
(f) anything made or done by the council, if in force on the appointed
day, is to have effect as if made or done by the Chapter so far as
that is required for continuing its effect after the appointed day;
(g) a bequest or other gift which is expressed as a gift to the council,
and which takes effect on or after the appointed day, is to take effect
as a gift to the Chapter.
15 Transfer of property of Vicar and Wardens
(1) On the appointed day all property which immediately before the
appointed day was vested in, held by, or held on trust for the incumbent
of the Cathedral benefice jointly with the churchwardens of the Cathedral
parish is transferred to the Chapter.
(2) Subsection (1) does not apply to —
(a) a parish burial ground, or
c SM 1 of 2025 Page 11

Section 16 Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025
(b) any other property vested in the incumbent and churchwardens for
the purpose of any functions (under the Burials Act 1986 or
otherwise) relating to parish burial grounds.
16 Transfer of property: general
Any property transferred by section 13, 14 or 15 vests in the Chapter without any
conveyance or other assurance, for the like purposes, upon the like trusts and
subject to the like conditions (if any) as those for, upon or subject to which it was
held immediately before the appointed day.
Supplemental
17 Guidance
A person on whom a function is conferred by or under this Measure may, in
exercising that function, have regard to any relevant guidance issued by the
Church Commissioners for England under the following provisions of the
Cathedrals Measure 2021, so far as applicable to the Cathedral —
(a) section 5(6) (finance);
(b) section 9(7) (reviews);
(c) section 10(6) (Visitor);
(d) section 12(8) (accountability);
(e) section 16(8) (Finance Committee).
18 Modification of certain legislation
The enactments specified in Schedule 3 are modified in relation to the Cathedral
in accordance with that Schedule.
19 Saving for certain powers
Nothing in this Measure affects —
(a) the powers of the Church Commissioners under the Mission and
Pastoral Measure (Isle of Man) 2012;
(b) the powers of the Board under Schedule 3 to the Church Act 1992
(ecclesiastical trusts); or
(c) the powers of the Attorney General or the High Court in relation to
charities.
20 Consequential amendments and repeals
(1) Schedule 4 (which makes consequential amendments) has effect.
(2) The enactments specified in Schedule 5 are repealed to the extent specified
in column 3 of that Schedule.

Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025 SCHEDULE 1
SCHEDULE 1
CONSTITUTION
[Section 5]
1 Constitution of the Chapter
(1) The Church Commissioners must, by an instrument under their common
seal, make a constitution for the Chapter.
(2) Before making a constitution the Church Commissioners must consult the
Chapter.
This requirement does not apply to the first constitution made under this
paragraph.
(3) The constitution shall not have effect unless it is approved by the Diocesan
Synod.
(4) The constitution is declared to be a statutory document.
2 Membership
(1) The constitution must provide as follows.
(2) The Chapter is to consist of such number of members, being not more
than 12, as is specified in the constitution.
(3) The members of the Chapter shall be, —
(a) the Dean,
(b) such number of canons of the Cathedral, being clerks in Holy
Orders, as is specified in the constitution (not being less than 2);
(c) such number of canons of the Cathedral, being lay persons, as is
specified in the constitution (not being less than 2);
(d) the churchwardens of the Cathedral parish;
(e) 2 persons elected in accordance with paragraph 5;
(f) such other persons as may be appointed by the Bishop.
3 The Dean
(1) The constitution must provide that the incumbent of the Cathedral
benefice is the Dean ex officio.
(2) The constitution must provide that if —
(a) the Cathedral benefice is vacant, or
(b) the Dean is unable to discharge his or her functions as such because
of illness, absence or any other reason,
c SM 1 of 2025 Page 13

SCHEDULE 1 Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025
the Bishop must appoint a clerk in Holy Orders who is qualified to hold
the Cathedral benefice (an “acting dean”) to carry out the functions
concerned during the period concerned.
(3) The constitution must specify the terms on which an acting dean may be
appointed.
4 Canons
(1) The constitution must provide for —
(a) the appointment by the Bishop of canons under paragraph 2(3)(b)
and (c);
(b) the tenure of office of canons;
(c) every canon in Holy Orders to be subject to the episcopal
jurisdiction of the Bishop.
(2) The constitution may provide for a canon to be appointed, with the
consent of the Board, on Common Tenure.
(3) In relation to a canon so appointed, any provision under sub-paragraph
(1)(b) has effect subject to the Ecclesiastical Offices (Terms of Service)
Measure 2009 and regulations made under it.
5 Elected members
The constitution must provide that —
(a) the elected members shall be elected by the persons whose names
are included on the church electoral roll of the Cathedral parish or
the community roll of the Cathedral;
(b) the Church Representation Rules shall apply to the election and
tenure of office of the elected members as they apply to the election
and tenure of office of representatives of the laity on a parochial
church council, subject to such modifications as are specified in the
constitution.
6 Disqualification
(1) This paragraph applies to members of the Chapter under
paragraph 2(3)(b), (c), (e) or (f).
(2) The constitution must provide that a person may not be appointed or
elected or serve as a member of the Chapter if he or she, —
(a) is included in a barred list;
(b) has been convicted of an offence mentioned in Schedule 1 to the
Children and Young Persons Act 1966;
(c) is disqualified from being a charity trustee under section 25(1) of
the Charities Registration and Regulation Act 2019 (unless the
Page 14 SM 1 of 2025 c

Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025 SCHEDULE 1
disqualification is subject to a waiver under section 25(4) of that
Act, either generally or in relation to the Chapter);
(d) is under the age of 18;
(e) in the case of a lay person, is not an actual communicant; or
(f) is a chief officer.
(3) The constitution must provide that, if a member of the Chapter is or
becomes disqualified under sub-paragraph (1), his or her seat is vacated.
(4) In this paragraph —
(a) “actual communicant” has the same meaning as in the Church
Representation Rules;
(b) rule 71(2) of those Rules applies to the reference to an offence in
sub-paragraph (2)(b).
7 Suspension
The constitution must provide for —
(a) the suspension by the Bishop of a lay member of the Chapter
appointed or elected under paragraph 2(3)(c), (e) or (f) in the like
circumstances as those in which a member of a parochial church
council may be suspended under rule 69 of the Church
Representation Rules;
(b) the revocation of such a suspension; and
(c) a right of appeal to the Vicar General against such a suspension.
8 Committees of the Chapter
(1) The constitution must make provision for the establishment of a Finance
Committee of the Chapter.
(2) The constitution may make provision for the establishment of other
committees of the Chapter.
(3) The constitution must provide that any committee of the Chapter
established by virtue of this paragraph may include persons who are not
members of the Chapter.
9 Advisory bodies
(1) The constitution may include provision for the establishment of one or
more advisory bodies.
(2) A body established under this paragraph is not a committee of the
Chapter.
c SM 1 of 2025 Page 15

SCHEDULE 1 Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025
10 Community roll
The constitution must provide for the formation and maintenance of a roll which
contains the name of each person, —
(a) who is not eligible for inclusion on the church electoral roll of the
Cathedral parish, but
(b) who is a member of the Cathedral community, and
(c) whose application for enrolment for the purposes of this paragraph
has been granted.
Page 16 SM 1 of 2025 c

Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025 SCHEDULE 2
SCHEDULE 2
STATUTES
[Section 6]
1 Duty to make statutes
(1) The Church Commissioners must, by an instrument under their common
seal, make statutes for the good government of the Cathedral.
(2) Before making statutes under this paragraph the Church Commissioners
must consult the Chapter.
This requirement does not apply to the first statutes made under this
paragraph.
(3) The statutes shall not have effect unless they are approved by the Bishop.
(4) The statutes are declared to be a statutory document.
2 Content of the statutes
(1) The statutes must include provision —
(a) as to the functions of the members and officers of the Chapter; and
(b) for the fostering of the corporate and spiritual life of the Chapter
and its members.
(2) The statutes may include consequential, supplementary, incidental or
transitional provision.
(3) Provision made in the statutes must be consistent with this Measure and
with the constitution.
3 Meetings of the Chapter
The statutes must provide for —
(a) the holding of a sufficient number of meetings of the Chapter to
enable the efficient transaction of its business;
(b) the conduct of meetings of the Chapter;
(c) the chairing of such meetings by the Dean or, if he or she is absent,
a member chosen by the members of the Chapter; and
(d) the attendance at such meetings of any officers of the Chapter at the
request of the Chapter.
4 Finance Committee
(1) The statutes must make provision for the composition, functions and
proceedings of the Finance Committee.
c SM 1 of 2025 Page 17

SCHEDULE 2 Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025
(2) The statutes must provide that —
(a) the members of the Finance Committee (who may include persons
who are not members of the Chapter) are to be appointed by the
Chapter;
(b) the Dean must not be a member of the Finance Committee but may
attend any meeting of the Committee and speak but may not vote;
(c) the chair of the Finance Committee is to be appointed by the
Chapter and must have recent and relevant financial experience;
(d) the terms of reference of the Finance Committee are to be set by the
Chapter; and
(e) the Finance Committee must keep under review the activities and
management of the Cathedral in relation to such matters as the
Chapter may specify in its terms of reference.
(3) The terms of reference of the Finance Committee may include provision
which supplements the provision made by the constitution or statutes.
5 Other committees
(1) The statutes must include provision as to the chairing of each committee
established by virtue of paragraph 8 of Schedule 1 (including provision as
to whether the chair must be a member of the Chapter).
(2) The statutes must include provision for each such committee to report its
proceedings to the Chapter.
(3) Subject to sub-paragraphs (1) and (2), the statutes must include provision
as to the composition and proceedings of such committees.
(4) The Chapter must set the terms of reference of each such committee; and
the terms of reference, —
(a) must include provision as to the functions of the committee;
(b) may include provision which supplements the provision made by
the constitution or statutes.
6 Chief officers
(1) The statutes must make provision for the appointment by the Chapter
of —
(a) an executive officer, and
(b) a financial officer,
by such titles, and with such functions, as are specified in the statutes.
(2) The statutes must require those officers to have recent experience relevant
to the exercise of their respective functions.
Page 18 SM 1 of 2025 c

Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025 SCHEDULE 2
7 Management group
(1) The statutes must include provision for the establishment of a group
concerned with the management of the Cathedral.
(2) The statutes must make provision as to —
(a) the composition of the group (which must include the Dean and
the chief officers);
(b) the functions and proceedings of the group, and
(c) the accountability of the group to the Chapter.
8 Advisory bodies
(1) The statutes may include provision as to the composition, functions or
proceedings of an advisory body established under paragraph 9 of
Schedule 1.
(2) The Chapter may set terms of reference for such a body; and the terms of
reference may include provision which supplements the provision made
by the constitution or statutes.
(3) Sub-paragraphs (1) and (2) are not to be read as limiting the provision
which may be made under paragraph 1 for the good government of the
Cathedral.
9 The Dean’s consent
(1) The statutes must provide that none of the following may be done without
the consent of the Dean, —
(a) an alteration of the ordering of services in the Cathedral;
(b) the settlement of the Chapter’s budget;
(c) the implementation of a decision taken by the Chapter in the
Dean’s absence.
(2) The statutes may contain provision as to the circumstances in which the
Dean is to be taken as having consented for the purposes of sub-paragraph
(1)(c).
10 Use of the Cathedral
The statutes must include provision as to the exercise by the Bishop of the rights
conferred by section 7(2).
11 Conferring of titles
(1) The statutes may enable the Bishop —
c SM 1 of 2025 Page 19

SCHEDULE 2 Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025
(a) to confer upon a canon who vacates his or her office and
immediately on such vacation retires, the title of canon emeritus;
and
(b) to confer upon any person (whether or not in Holy Orders) the title
of honorary canon, for such period and on such terms as may be
specified in the statutes.
(2) The statutes must provide that a person who, immediately before the
appointed day, held a canonry in the Chapter shall hold the title of
honorary canon unless and until he or she —
(a) would have ceased to hold that canonry if this Measure had not
been passed, or
(b) is appointed a canon under paragraph 4 of Schedule 1.
(3) A title conferred under this paragraph does not confer on the holder any
office or benefice or any right to membership of the Chapter or of any other
body in the Cathedral.
Page 20 SM 1 of 2025 c

SCHEDULE 3
MODIFICATION OF CERTAIN LEGISLATION
[Section 18]
1 Church Representation Rules
(1) In the application of the Rules to the Cathedral parish —
(a) omit rules M1(3)(d), M5(4) to (8) and M6(1)(b), (2) and (7);
(b) in rule M8, omit the reference to the parochial church council;
(c) omit rules M15 to M42; and
(d) despite Rule M1(2)(b) and (3), every clerk in Holy Orders holding
office in the Cathedral is entitled to attend any annual, special or
extraordinary parochial church meeting of the parish and to take
part in its proceedings, whether or not he or she is resident in the
parish.
(2) The above modifications are subject to any provision made under
paragraph 5(b) of Schedule 1.
2 Parochial Church Councils (Powers) Measure 1956
In the application of the Measure to the Cathedral parish, omit sections 3, 3A, 6,
8 and 9.
3 Patronage (Benefices) Measure 1986
(1) In its application to the Cathedral benefice the Measure is modified as
follows.
(2) In relation to the Cathedral parish —
(a) the functions of the parochial church council and the secretary of
the council are exercisable by the Chapter and the chapter clerk
respectively;
(b) section 11(1)(b), (5) and (6) does not apply;
(c) the elected members of the Chapter are the parish representatives.
(3) In relation to the parish of the West Coast section 11(1)(b) and (5) applies
without the modifications in Schedule 2.
4 Clergy Discipline Measure 2003
Where the Cathedral parish has a proper interest in making a complaint under
section 10, subsection (1) of that section has effect with the substitution for
paragraph (a) of the following:
«(a) in the case of a priest or deacon, by —
c SM 1 of 2025 Page 21

SCHEDULE 3 Cathedral Measure (Isle of Man) 2025
(i) a person nominated by the Chapter, or
(ii) a churchwarden of the parish, or
(iii) any other person who has a proper interest in
making the complaint;».
5 Ecclesiastical Offices (Terms of Service) Measure 2009
Section 1(1) has effect as if it included a reference to the office held by a canon
whose appointment is expressed to be on Common Tenure under paragraph 4(2)
of Schedule 1.
6 Mission and Pastoral Measure (Isle of Man) 2012
A pastoral scheme affecting the Cathedral, the Cathedral benefice or the
Cathedral parish may make such amendments or modifications of the
constitution or the statutes as the Church Commissioners think necessary or
expedient.
7 Ecclesiastical Jurisdiction and Care of Churches Measure 2018
(1) In sections 49, 50 and 53, for each reference to the churchwardens
substitute a reference to the Chapter.
(2) In sections 60(1)(b) and 78(6), for the references to the minister and
churchwardens substitute references to the Chapter.

MEASURE (ISLE OF MAN) 2025
Approved by Sodor and Man Diocesan Synod: 10 July 2024
Approved by Tynwald: 22 January 2025
Received Royal Assent: 8 July 2025
Announced to Tynwald: 14 July 2025
A MEASURE enacted pursuant to the Church Legislation Procedure Act 1993 to
extend to the Isle of Man the Church of England (Miscellaneous Provisions)
Measure 2024; to repeal obsolete provisions relating to sequestration; and for
connected purposes.
1 Short Title
The short title of this Measure is the Church (Miscellaneous Provisions) Measure
(Isle of Man) 2025.
2 Commencement
(1) This Measure comes into operation on such day or days as the Bishop may
by order appoint.1
(2) An order under subsection (1) may include such transitional provisions as
appear to the Bishop to be necessary or expedient for the purposes of the
order.
3 Extension of Church of England (Miscellaneous Provisions)
Measure 2024 to the Isle of Man
The following provisions of the Church of England (Miscellaneous Provisions)
Measure 2024 extend to the Island subject to the exceptions, adaptations and
modifications specified in Schedule 1 —
section 3 (safeguarding code revisions);
section 6 (delegation of episcopal functions);
section 9 (judges: appointment etc.);
c SM 2 of 2025 Page 5

Section 4 Church (Miscellaneous Provisions) Measure (Isle of Man) 2025

section 12 (live broadcast of proceedings);

section 20 and Schedule 2 (meetings);
section 22 (short title etc.).
4 Amendment of Churchwardens Measure
In section 3 (interpretation) of the Churchwardens Measure (Isle of Man) 2013 —
(a) at the beginning insert «(1)»;
(b) at the end insert, —
«(2) A reference in this Measure to a meeting of parishioners includes a
reference to a meeting which persons may attend, speak at, vote in
or otherwise participate in without any, or without all, of the
persons being together in the same place.
(3) A reference in this Measure to the place where a meeting of
parishioners is to be held includes a reference to more than one
place, including electronic, digital or virtual locations, web
addresses or conference call telephone numbers.
(4) A person is to be regarded for the purposes of this Measure as
present at a meeting of parishioners at any given time if the person
is at that time able to hear and be heard by, and where practicable
to see and be seen by, the other persons present.
(5) A reference in this Measure to being present at a meeting of
parishioners includes a reference to being present by electronic
means, including by telephone conference, video conference, live
webcast or live interactive streaming.».
5 Sequestration
(1) The enactments specified in Part 1 of Schedule 2 are amended in
accordance with that Part.
(2) The enactments specified in Part 2 of Schedule 2 are repealed to the extent
specified in column 2 of that Part.

of Man) 1997, in section 5 of the Patronage (Benefices) Measure 1986, after
subsection (3) insert».

GAS REGULATION (AMENDMENT) ACT 2024
Signed in Tynwald: 16 January 2024
Received Royal Assent: 16 January 2024
Announced to Tynwald: 16 January 2024
AN ACT to amend section 12 of the Gas Regulation Act 1995 to confer power on
the Communications and Utilities Regulatory Authority to require persons to
produce documents and furnish information to it for the purposes of the setting
of parameters within which a public gas supplier may fix tariffs (and to make
consequential amendments); to amend Schedule 2 to that Act to confer on the
Council of Ministers the power to make orders to specify circumstances where
non-payment by a tariff customer may not lead to the cutting off of the supply of
gas to premises; and for connected purposes.
BE IT ENACTED by the King’s Most Excellent Majesty, by and with the advice and
consent of the Council and Keys in Tynwald assembled, and by the authority of the
same, as follows: —
1 Short title
The short title of this Act is the Gas Regulation (Amendment) Act 2024.
2 Interpretation
(1) In this Act, “the principal Act” means the Gas Regulation Act 1995.
(2) References in this Act to a numbered section or Schedule are, in each case,
to the section or Schedule so numbered in the principal Act.
